content.text: describes any task where both the input and output are variable-length sequences. The input and output lengths are independent — a long article can summarise to a single sentence, and a short question can expand into a multi-paragraph answer. This is fundamentally different from classification, where the output is always a fixed label.
The classic neural architecture for seq2seq is the
: the encoder reads the entire input and compresses it into a representation; the decoder generates the output one token at a time, conditioned on that representation.

attrs.alt: Encoder-decoder architecture diagram with French input tokens flowing through encoder to context vector to decoder to English output tokens, plus four common seq2seq task boxes below

content.text: , the model is trained to maximise:

attrs.latex: \log P(Y \mid X) = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \log P(y_t \mid y_1, \ldots, y_{t-1}, X)

content.text: The decoder is autoregressive: it generates one token at a time, conditioning on all previously generated tokens
the full source via cross-attention. During training the true target tokens are fed in ("teacher forcing"); at inference the model's own predictions are fed back in.
T5 ("Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer") frames every NLP task as a seq2seq problem by prepending a task prefix to the input.

from transformers import T5ForConditionalGeneration, T5Tokenizer

model_name = "t5-small"
tokenizer = T5Tokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = T5ForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained(model_name)

# Translation: prefix tells T5 which task to perform
inputs = tokenizer(
    "translate English to French: The cat is sleeping.",
    return_tensors="pt",
)
output_ids = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=50)
print(tokenizer.decode(output_ids[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
# Le chat dort.

# Summarisation: same model, different prefix
inputs = tokenizer(
    "summarize: The quick brown fox jumped over the lazy dog "
    "and then ran off into the sunset.",
    return_tensors="pt",
)
output_ids = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=30)
print(tokenizer.decode(output_ids[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
# the fox jumped over the lazy dog.
For seq2seq tasks with a definite correct answer (translation, summarisation),
is the standard decoding strategy. It maintains B candidate sequences ("beams") and keeps the B highest-scoring ones at each step. Greedy decoding is beam search with B=1.

from transformers import MarianMTModel, MarianTokenizer

# Helsinki-NLP models are encoder-decoder specifically for translation
model_name = "Helsinki-NLP/opus-mt-fr-en"
tokenizer = MarianTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = MarianMTModel.from_pretrained(model_name)

src = ["Le chat dort sur le tapis.", "Le chien mange."]
tokenized = tokenizer(src, return_tensors="pt", padding=True)

translated = model.generate(
    **tokenized,
    num_beams=4,           # beam width — explore 4 candidates
    early_stopping=True,   # stop when all beams reach EOS
    max_length=50,
)
for t in translated:
    print(tokenizer.decode(t, skip_special_tokens=True))
# The cat sleeps on the rug.
# The dog eats.
Full encoder + causal decoder with cross-attention

Modern large language models are increasingly decoder-only even for seq2seq tasks — the source is prepended to the prompt and the model generates the target as a continuation. This works well in practice but loses the bidirectional encoding of the source that a true encoder-decoder model provides.
— measures n-gram overlap between generated and reference translations. Standard for machine translation but known to correlate poorly with human judgement at the sentence level.
(Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation) — measures recall of n-grams from the reference in the generated output. Standard for summarisation (ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L).
— compares contextual embeddings of generated and reference tokens using cosine similarity. More robust to paraphrases than n-gram metrics.

from evaluate import load

bleu = load("bleu")
rouge = load("rouge")

predictions = ["The cat sleeps on the rug."]
references  = [["The cat is sleeping on the mat."]]

print(bleu.compute(predictions=predictions, references=references))
# {'bleu': 0.356, ...}

print(rouge.compute(predictions=predictions, references=["The cat is sleeping on the mat."]))
# {'rouge1': 0.727, 'rouge2': 0.5, 'rougeL': 0.727, ...}

Sequence-to-sequence tasks represent a large fraction of real-world NLP applications — translation, summarisation, question answering, and code generation together account for the majority of commercial language model deployments. Understanding the unified seq2seq framing is practically important: it explains why a single architecture like T5 can handle diverse tasks with one shared set of parameters, and why evaluation metrics (BLEU, ROUGE) measure what they do and fail in the ways they do.

The seq2seq formulation also clarifies the difference between tasks that require compression (summarisation, where output is shorter than input), tasks that require transformation (translation, where output has similar length in a different vocabulary), and tasks that require generation (code, essays, where output may be longer than the prompt). Each variant imposes different demands on the decoder's strategy and on how you measure success.
The text-to-text framing of T5 lets one model handle translation, summarisation, classification, and QA with a task prefix, reducing the model inventory needed in production.
Unlike classification heads that produce fixed-size outputs, seq2seq decoders generate sequences of any length, making them suitable for open-ended generation tasks.
Span corruption (T5) and denoising (BART) objectives train both the encoder's understanding and the decoder's generation capability simultaneously.
The encoder attends to the full source sequence in both directions, giving the decoder richer source representations than a decoder-only model's causal prefix.
An encoder-decoder model has roughly twice as many parameters as an equivalently sized encoder-only or decoder-only model, increasing memory requirements.
Autoregressive decoding generates one token at a time, making inference O(n) in the output length. Long outputs (essays, code) are substantially slower than short ones.
Maintaining B candidate sequences at each step multiplies the decoding cost by B. Beam search with B=4 costs 4x more than greedy decoding.
BLEU and ROUGE measure n-gram overlap, not semantic quality. A paraphrase with identical meaning but different wording scores poorly.
Classic use case; encoder-decoder outperforms decoder-only at similar parameter counts
Encoder reads full document; decoder generates novel text
Span selection is better handled by encoder-only with a span head
Encoder-only is more parameter-efficient for fixed-label classification
Seq2seq for spec→code; decoder-only (GPT style) for open-ended generation
Large decoder-only models match or exceed seq2seq for open-ended dialogue
Sequence labelling with encoder-only is more direct
The following example fine-tunes T5-small on a summarisation task using the Hugging Face Trainer. The same pattern applies to translation and QA by changing the prefix and dataset.

from datasets import load_dataset
from transformers import (
    T5Tokenizer, T5ForConditionalGeneration,
    Seq2SeqTrainer, Seq2SeqTrainingArguments, DataCollatorForSeq2Seq
)

tokenizer = T5Tokenizer.from_pretrained("t5-small")
model     = T5ForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained("t5-small")

# CNN/DailyMail is the standard summarisation benchmark
dataset = load_dataset("cnn_dailymail", "3.0.0")

PREFIX = "summarize: "
MAX_SRC = 512
MAX_TGT = 128

def preprocess(batch):
    inputs  = tokenizer(
        [PREFIX + doc for doc in batch["article"]],
        max_length=MAX_SRC, truncation=True, padding="max_length"
    )
    targets = tokenizer(
        batch["highlights"],
        max_length=MAX_TGT, truncation=True, padding="max_length"
    )
    # Replace padding token ids of targets with -100 so they are ignored in loss
    labels = [
        [(t if t != tokenizer.pad_token_id else -100) for t in tgt]
        for tgt in targets["input_ids"]
    ]
    inputs["labels"] = labels
    return inputs

tokenized = dataset.map(preprocess, batched=True, remove_columns=dataset["train"].column_names)

args = Seq2SeqTrainingArguments(
    output_dir="t5-cnn-summary",
    num_train_epochs=1,
    per_device_train_batch_size=8,
    per_device_eval_batch_size=8,
    predict_with_generate=True,
    evaluation_strategy="epoch",
    save_strategy="epoch",
    load_best_model_at_end=True,
    fp16=True,            # requires GPU
)

collator = DataCollatorForSeq2Seq(tokenizer, model=model, label_pad_token_id=-100)

trainer = Seq2SeqTrainer(
    model=model,
    args=args,
    train_dataset=tokenized["train"].select(range(5000)),   # small subset for demo
    eval_dataset=tokenized["validation"].select(range(500)),
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
    data_collator=collator,
)

trainer.train()

A legal-tech company built a contract clause extraction system using BART (a BERT-encoder + GPT-decoder seq2seq model). The system reads long contract documents and outputs short, standardised descriptions of each clause. The key engineering decisions were: (1) truncate input to the maximum context window (1024 tokens) with a sliding window and merge overlapping outputs; (2) use beam search with no-repeat-ngram-size=3 to reduce redundant phrases; (3) evaluate with ROUGE-L rather than ROUGE-1 to capture clause-level completeness.

from transformers import BartTokenizer, BartForConditionalGeneration
import textwrap

tokenizer = BartTokenizer.from_pretrained("facebook/bart-large-cnn")
model     = BartForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained("facebook/bart-large-cnn")

def extract_clause_summary(clause_text: str, max_words: int = 60) -> str:
    """Summarise a legal clause to at most max_words words."""
    inputs = tokenizer(
        clause_text,
        max_length=1024, truncation=True, return_tensors="pt"
    )
    summary_ids = model.generate(
        inputs["input_ids"],
        num_beams=4,
        no_repeat_ngram_size=3,
        length_penalty=2.0,
        min_length=20,
        max_length=max_words * 2,   # tokens ≈ words * 1.3
        early_stopping=True,
    )
    return tokenizer.decode(summary_ids[0], skip_special_tokens=True)

sample_clause = textwrap.dedent("""
    9.1 Confidentiality. Each party (the "Receiving Party") shall maintain
    the strict confidentiality of the other party's (the "Disclosing Party's")
    Confidential Information and shall not disclose such Confidential Information
    to any third party without the Disclosing Party's prior written consent.
    The Receiving Party shall use the Confidential Information solely for the
    purposes of this Agreement and shall protect such information with at least
    the same degree of care as it uses to protect its own confidential information
    but in no event less than reasonable care.
""")

print(extract_clause_summary(sample_clause))
# "Each party must keep the other party's confidential information secret and
#  may only use it for the purposes of this Agreement with at least reasonable care."
Using BLEU as the sole metric:

BLEU measures surface-form n-gram overlap. A perfectly correct paraphrase scores near zero. Use BLEU alongside BERTScore and human evaluation for translation; ROUGE alongside METEOR and semantic similarity for summarisation.
T5 requires the correct prefix ('summarize: ', 'translate English to French: ') to activate the right behaviour. Omitting it produces gibberish or off-task output.
Training uses teacher forcing (feeding the true previous token); inference must use the model's own predictions. Using the wrong mode in evaluation inflates scores artificially.

Feeding sequences longer than the model's maximum position encoding (512 for T5-small, 1024 for BART) silently truncates in some implementations and raises errors in others. Always set truncation=True.
Greedy decoding is fast but produces lower-quality translations than beam search with B≥4. Always use beam search in production translation systems.
Use length_penalty in beam search to control the desired output length: values > 1.0 encourage longer outputs; < 1.0 encourage shorter ones.
For long documents, use a sliding window: split the document into overlapping chunks, summarise each, then concatenate and re-summarise the concatenated summaries.
Always evaluate with multiple metrics: BLEU/ROUGE for n-gram overlap, BERTScore for semantic similarity, and human evaluation for fluency and faithfulness.
Fine-tune rather than use off-the-shelf models for domain-specific tasks: legal, medical, and scientific language differs substantially from the news text T5 and BART were trained on.
Cache encoder representations when translating the same source into multiple languages — the encoder output is language-agnostic and can be reused for each target-language decoder call.

Encoder-decoder inference has two stages: (1) the encoder runs once over the source — O(n²) in source length due to self-attention, run once per request; (2) the decoder runs T times, once per output token — each step is O(m²) in decoder length but can be accelerated with a KV cache that stores previous key-value pairs. Total inference FLOPs scale roughly as O(n²L_enc + Tm²L_dec) where L is the number of layers.

In practice, for source lengths up to 512 tokens and output lengths up to 128 tokens, T5-base runs at ~50 ms per request on a single A100 GPU. BART-large-cnn runs at ~200 ms. For high-throughput production systems, batching multiple requests together is essential: a batch size of 16 typically gives 10x higher GPU utilisation than single-request inference, reducing per-request cost to ~5–20 ms.

Seq2seq models can generate hallucinated content — output that is fluent and plausible but factually wrong. This is especially dangerous in summarisation, where the model may invent facts not present in the source document. Mitigation strategies include: faithfulness scoring (checking that each claim in the summary is entailed by the source using an NLI model), retrieval-augmented generation to ground outputs in retrieved source documents, and factual consistency metrics like FactCC or SummaC.

For translation in regulated contexts (legal, medical), output should always be reviewed by a qualified human before use. Neural translation models can subtly distort meaning — especially for negations, numeric quantities, and conditional clauses — in ways that are difficult to detect without domain expertise.
Seq2seq covers any task where both input and output are variable-length sequences:
translation, summarisation, QA, code generation, dialogue.
provides bidirectional source encoding (encoder) and autoregressive target generation (decoder) connected by cross-attention.
T5 unifies all NLP tasks as text-to-text
using simple task prefixes, enabling one model to handle diverse tasks with a shared cross-entropy loss.
maximise the log-probability of each target token given all previous target tokens and the full source sequence.
Beam search is the standard decoder for structured tasks
like translation; sampling is preferred for open-ended generation.
BLEU and ROUGE measure n-gram overlap, not semantic quality —
always pair them with BERTScore or human evaluation.
controls the desired output length and should be tuned per task.
Hallucination is the main reliability risk:
use faithfulness scoring and human review in production summarisation systems.
